name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ يَهْدِى لِلَّتِى هِىَ أَقْوَمُ وَيُبَشِّرُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا كَبِيرًا (٩)
```

```english
Indeed this Quran guides to what is most upright, and gives the good news to the faithful who do righteous deeds that there is a great reward for them. (9)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٠)
```

```english
As for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have prepared a painful punishment for them. (10)
```

```arabic
وَيَدْعُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ بِٱلشَّرِّ دُعَآءَهُۥ بِٱلْخَيْرِ ۖ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَجُولًا (١١)
```

```english
Man prays for ill as [avidly as] he prays for good, and man is overhasty. (11)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ءَايَتَيْنِ ۖ فَمَحَوْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلنَّهَارِ مُبْصِرَةً لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَلِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ ۚ وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ تَفْصِيلًا (١٢)
```

```english
We made the night and the day two signs. Then We faded out the sign of the night, and made the sign of the day lightsome, so that you may seek from your Lord’s bounty and that you may know the number of years and calculation [of time], and We have elaborated everything in detail. (12)
```

```arabic
وَكُلَّ إِنسَـٰنٍ أَلْزَمْنَـٰهُ طَـٰٓئِرَهُۥ فِى عُنُقِهِۦ ۖ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ كِتَـٰبًا يَلْقَىٰهُ مَنشُورًا (١٣)
```

```english
We have attached every person’s omen to his neck, and We shall bring it out for him on the Day of Resurrection as a book that he will find wide open. (13)
```

```arabic
ٱقْرَأْ كِتَـٰبَكَ كَفَىٰ بِنَفْسِكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيبًا (١٤)
```

```english
‘Read your book! Today your soul suffices as your own reckoner.’ (14)
```

```arabic
مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا (١٥)
```

```english
Whoever is guided is guided only for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, goes astray only to its detriment. No bearer shall bear another’s burden. We do not punish [any community] until We have sent [it] an apostle. (15)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَآ أَرَدْنَآ أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا۟ فِيهَا فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا ٱلْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَـٰهَا تَدْمِيرًا (١٦)
```

```english
And when We desire to destroy a town We command its affluent ones [to obey Allah]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly. (16)
```

```arabic
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ نُوحٍ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا (١٧)
```

```english
How many generations We have destroyed since Noah! Your Lord is sufficient as [a witness who is] well aware and percipient of His servants’ sins. (17)
```

```arabic
مَّن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنَا لَهُۥ فِيهَا مَا نَشَآءُ لِمَن نُّرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا لَهُۥ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَىٰهَا مَذْمُومًا مَّدْحُورًا (١٨)
```

```english
Whoever desires this transitory life, We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blameful and spurned. (18)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ أَرَادَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَسَعَىٰ لَهَا سَعْيَهَا وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ سَعْيُهُم مَّشْكُورًا (١٩)
```

```english
Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an endeavour worthy of it, should he be faithful,—the endeavour of such will be well-appreciated. (19)
```

```arabic
كُلًّا نُّمِدُّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مِنْ عَطَآءِ رَبِّكَ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ عَطَآءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا (٢٠)
```

```english
To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined. (20)
```

```arabic
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَلَلْـَٔاخِرَةُ أَكْبَرُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ وَأَكْبَرُ تَفْضِيلًا (٢١)
```

```english
Observe how We have given some of them an advantage over some others; yet the Hereafter is surely greater in respect of ranks and greater in respect of relative merit. (21)
```

```arabic
لَّا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتَقْعُدَ مَذْمُومًا مَّخْذُولًا (٢٢)
```

```english
Do not set up another god besides Allah, or you will sit blameworthy, forsaken. (22)
```

## (Commentary)

The preceding group of verses described the unfolding of the Divine Sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — *the established way of Allah* — in guiding humanity toward the path of truth and the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. It then detailed the felicity of those who respond to the true call in this world and the Hereafter, and the punishment of those who disbelieve in the truth and transgress against the command in their worldly life and their final end. The mention of the revelation of the Torah and what subsequently befell the Children of Israel served as an example cited within the discourse to apply the universal rule (الحكم الكلي) to its individual instances and manifestations (مصاديقه). This current group of verses mentions the unfolding of the aforementioned Sunnah in this Ummah (الأمة) — *the community of believers* — just as it unfolded in the community of Moses. A conclusion has been drawn from the verses regarding the necessity of avoiding polytheism (الشرك) and the obligation of adhering to the path of monotheism, where it was said: **لا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ فَتَقْعُدَ مَذْمُوماً مَخْذُولاً** — "Do not set up another god besides Allah, or you will sit blamed and forsaken." (17:22)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ** — "Indeed this Quran guides to that which is most upright" (17:9); meaning to the creed (الملة) that is most upright, as the Exalted said: **قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدانِي رَبِّي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ دِيناً قِيَماً مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً** — "Say, ‘Indeed my Lord has guided me to a straight path, the upright religion, the creed of Abraham, a Hanif...’" (6:161).

The term **most upright** (الأقوم) is a superlative form (أفعل تفضيل). The root of the word is "standing" (القيام), the opposite of "sitting" (القعود), which is one of the states and postures of a human being. It is the most balanced of his states, through which he gains mastery over the actions he intends to perform, unlike sitting, lying on one's back, or lying prostrate. It was then used metaphorically to denote one's excellent management of affairs when one is capable of them without incapacity or fatigue, and one's optimal administration of them toward their goal. It is said: "He stood by such an affair" (قام بأمر كذا) when he took charge of it, and "He stood over such an affair" (قام على أمر كذا) meaning he monitored it, preserved it, and looked after its condition in a manner that suits it.

Allah, the Glorified, has described this primordial creed (الملة الحنيفية) with "standing" (القيام), as He said: **فأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** — "So set your heart on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah which He has created in mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion..." (30:30), and He said: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ** — "So set your heart on the upright religion..." (30:43).

This is because this religion is a guardian (مهيمناً) over that which contains the good of their worldly life and their Hereafter, standing (قيماً) over the rectification of their state in their livelihood and their return to Allah. This is only because it is in accordance with what is necessitated by the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and the creation upon which Allah, the Glorified, fashioned him and equipped him accordingly with that which guides him to the goal intended for him and the felicity prepared for his sake.

Accordingly, the description of this creed in His saying: **لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ** — "to that which is most upright" — as being "most upright," if compared to all other creeds, is from the perspective that each of those creeds is a life-system (سنة حيوية) adopted by people to benefit from in some of their life affairs. However, if it benefits them in some aspects, it harms them in others; and if it secures for them a portion of what accords with their whims, it causes them to miss a great portion of what constitutes their true good. It is only Islam that stands over their lives and all that concerns them in this world and the Hereafter without anything being lost. Thus, the primordial creed is more upright (أقوم) than any other regarding human life.

If the comparison is made to the other previous Divine legislations (الشرائع الإلهية), such as the legislation of Noah, Moses, and Jesus (peace be upon them) — as is apparent from making it that which the Quran guides to, in contrast to the previous mention of the Torah and its being made a guidance for the Children of Israel — then it is from the perspective that this primordial creed is more perfect than the previous creeds contained in the books of the previous prophets. It encompasses of Divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) the utmost that the human constitution can bear, and of legislations that which leaves no individual or social human action unaddressed. The Exalted has said: **وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَمُهَيْمِناً عَلَيْهِ** — "We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it." (5:48). Thus, that which the Quran guides to is more upright than that which other books guide to.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيُبَشِّرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ الصَّالِحاتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْراً كَبِيراً** — "and gives the good news to the faithful who do righteous deeds that there is a great reward for them" (17:9). "Righteous" (الصالحات) is an adjective whose noun is omitted for brevity; the implied meaning is "and they performed righteous deeds" (الأعمال الصالحات).

In the verse, a right (حق) is established for the believers who have attained faith and performed righteous deeds (الصالحات) — *actions that are inherently good and performed in accordance with Divine command* — upon Allah, the Glorified, as supported by naming it a "reward" (أجراً). This is also supported by His saying in another place: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ** — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there is for them an undiminished reward." (41:8). There is no problem in them having a right upon Allah if Allah, the Glorified, is the one who established it. A similar instance is His saying: **ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كَذلِكَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نُنْجِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "Then We shall deliver Our apostles and those who have faith. Thus it is a duty incumbent upon Us to deliver the faithful." (10:103).

The focus in the verse is on clarifying the fulfilled promise (الوعد المنجز), just as the following verse is concerned with clarifying the fulfilled threat (الوعيد المنجز), which is the punishment for those who disbelieve in the Hereafter. As for the one who believes but does not perform righteous deeds, he is not among those who have a fulfilled reward and a fixed right upon Allah; rather, his matter is deferred (مراعى) pending repentance or intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — until he joins the company of the righteous among the believers. The Exalted said: **وَآخَرُونَ اعْتَرَفُوا بِذُنُوبِهِمْ خَلَطُوا عَمَلاً صالِحاً وَآخَرَ سَيِّئاً عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "And [there are] others who have confessed to their sins: they have mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil. Maybe Allah will accept their repentance." (9:102), and He said: **وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "And [there are] others waiting for Allah’s decree: either He shall punish them, or He shall accept their repentance." (9:106).

Indeed, they possess a firmness upon the truth by virtue of their faith, as the Exalted said: **وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** — "and give good news to those who have faith that they have a standing of sincerity with their Lord." (10:2), and He said: **يُثَبِّتُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** — "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter." (14:27).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ أَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً** — "and that for those who do not believe in the Hereafter We have prepared a painful punishment." (17:10). "Preparation" (الاعتاد) means to make ready and set aside, derived from "equipment" (العتاد). According to what al-Raghib mentioned, it is the storing of something before the need for it arises, similar to "preparation" (الإعداد).

The apparent context (السياق) is that it is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: **أَنَّ لَهُمْ** — "that for them..." — etc. Thus, the implied meaning is: "and He gives the good news to the believers who do righteous deeds that those who do not believe..." etc. This being "good news" for the believers is from the perspective that it is a Divine retribution against their enemies in religion.

The mention was specifically restricted to their lack of belief in the Hereafter, despite the possibility of their disbelieving in other things such as monotheism and prophethood, because the discourse is directed toward clarifying the effect that follows the upright religion. There is no place for religion, nor any benefit in it, alongside the denial of the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and final judgment* — even if one were to acknowledge the oneness of the Lord, the Exalted, and other matters of gnosis. For this reason, the Glorified counted the forgetting of the Day of Reckoning as the root of all misguidance in His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ** — "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because of their forgetting the Day of Reckoning." (38:26).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَدْعُ الْإِنْسانُ بِالشَّرِّ دُعاءَهُ بِالْخَيْرِ وَكانَ الْإِنْسانُ عَجُولاً** — "Man prays for evil as [vehemently as] he prays for good, and man is ever hasty." (17:11). What is meant by "prayer" (الدعاء), as inferred from the context, is absolute seeking (مطلق الطلب), whether it be through the verbal form of prayer, such as saying: "O Allah, provide me with wealth and children," and so on, or without a verbal prayer, but rather through seeking and striving. All of that is a prayer and a request from Allah, whether the human being believes in it and is conscious of it or not, since there is no giver or withholder in reality except Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted said: **يَسْئَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — "Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him." (55:29), and He said: **وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ** — "And He gave you all that you had asked Him." (14:34). Thus, prayer is absolute seeking. The preposition "with" (بـ) in His saying **بِالشَّرِّ** (for evil) and **بِالْخَيْرِ** (for good) is for connection (للصلة), and the meaning is that man calls for evil and requests it just as he calls for good, requests it, and seeks it.

Accordingly, the meaning of man being "hasty" (عجولاً) is that he does not adopt deliberation (الأناة) when he desires something so that he might reflect and think upon the aspects of its benefit and harm until the face of good in the matter he desires becomes clear to him, and he then seeks it and strives toward it. Rather, he hastens in seeking it as soon as he remembers it and his whim attaches to it; thus, it may be evil and he is harmed by it, or it may be good and he benefits from it.

The verse and the verses following it are in the context of reproach and blame, branching out from what preceded regarding the Divine favor of guidance to that which is most upright. It is as if it were said: We have sent down a Book that guides to a creed that is most upright, leading those who take it to felicity and Paradise and bringing them to a great reward, and directing them to all good. However, the human species is hasty; due to his haste, he does not distinguish between good and evil, but rather seeks everything that appears to him and asks for everything that occurs to him and to which his whim attaches, without distinguishing between good and evil, or truth and falsehood. Thus, he pursues evil as he pursues good and rushes toward falsehood as he rushes toward truth.

It is not appropriate for him to be hasty and seek everything he desires and craves, nor is it right for him to commit everything he has the capacity to commit and perpetrate everything Allah has empowered him to do and enabled him for, based on the argument that it is from Divine facilitation and that had He willed, He would have prevented him. For these are the night and the day, two Divine signs (آيتان إلهيتان), and they are not of the same pattern. Rather, the sign of the night is "effaced" (ممحوة), wherein movements cease and eyes rest, and the sign of the day is "luminous" (مبصرة), wherein faculties awaken and people seek of the bounty of their Lord and through it know the number of years and the reckoning.

Similarly, the deeds of evil and good all exist in being by the permission of Allah, within the power of man through the empowerment of the Glorified. However, that is not a proof for the permissibility of man committing both evil and good, nor for him to be hasty and seek everything that appears to him, pursuing evil as he pursues good and committing disobedience as he commits obedience. Rather, it is obligatory upon him to take the deed of evil as "effaced" and thus not commit it, and the deed of good as "luminous" and thus perform it, seeking thereby the bounty of his Lord in the felicity of the Hereafter and the noble provision. For the deed of man is his "bird" (طائره) — *his omen or destiny* — which points him toward his felicity or wretchedness, and it is inseparable from him. Whatever he performs of good or evil does not leave him for another, nor is anyone else guided by it.

This is what the context provides for the meaning of the verse, and through it, several points become clear:

First: That the verse and the verses following it are directed toward the purpose of reproach and blame. This is the aspect of their connection, and the connection of what follows them, to what preceded in His saying: **إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ** — "Indeed this Quran guides to that which is most upright" — as we indicated just now. Thus, the Glorified blames man because, due to his innate disposition toward haste, he does not appreciate the blessing of Divine guidance as it deserves, nor does he distinguish between the creed that is most upright and others; thus, he prays for evil as he prays for good and intends wretchedness as he intends felicity.

Second: That what is meant by "man" (الإنسان) is the species, not specific individuals such as the disbelievers and polytheists, as has been suggested. By "prayer" (الدعاء) is meant absolute seeking, not the technical prayer, as has been suggested. By "good" and "evil" is meant that which contains the felicity of his life or its wretchedness in reality, not absolutely everything that harms or benefits, such as a person praying for the success and prosperity of one with whom he is pleased, and for the failure and loss of one with whom he is angry, and so on.

By "haste" (العجلة) is meant the human desire for what he loves to be realized quickly, not persistence and perseverance in seeking punishment and the disliked.

The commentators have a strange disagreement regarding the individual words of the verse, and confused statements in explaining the aspect of the connection of the verse, as well as the following verses, to what preceded. We have omitted mentioning them or delving into them due to the lack of benefit in addressing them; whoever desires them may refer to their books.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ آيَتَيْنِ فَمَحَوْنا آيَةَ اللَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنا آيَةَ النَّهارِ مُبْصِرَةً** — "We made the night and the day two signs. Then We effaced the sign of the night and made the sign of the day luminous..." (17:12) to the end of the verse. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "**Luminous** (مبصرة) means radiant, illuminating, and bright. Abu Amr said: He intended that one sees by means of it, just as it is said: 'a sleeping night' (ليل نائم) and 'a hiding secret' (سر كاتم). Al-Kisa'i said: The Arabs say: 'The day became luminous' (أبصر النهار) when it shone." This is the end of the relevant portion.

The night and the day are the light and darkness that alternate upon the earth due to the sun being faced at its rising and its disappearance at its setting. Like everything else in the universe of the essences of things and their states, they are two signs (آيتان) of Allah, the Glorified, pointing by their very essence to His uniqueness in Lordship (الربوبية).

From here it becomes clear that what is meant by making them two signs is creating them as such, not that they were created and were not signs, then He made them signs and clothed them in the garment of indication. For all things are signs of Him, the Exalted, from the perspective of the origin of their existence and their being, which points to their Originator, not due to an accidental attribute that occurs to them.

From here it also becomes clear that what is meant by the "sign of the night" is the night itself, just as the "sign of the day" is the day itself — on the basis that the genitive construction (الإضافة) is explanatory (بيانية), not possessive (لامية). What is meant by the "effacing" of the night is its darkening and its being hidden from sight, in contrast to the day.

Thus, what some have mentioned — that the "sign of the night" refers to the moon and its "effacing" refers to the spots seen on its face, just as the "sign of the day" refers to the sun and its being made "luminous" refers to its disk being free from effacement and blackness — is not sound. For the discourse is about the "two signs," not the "two signs of the two signs." Furthermore, what was branched from that in His saying: **لِتَبْتَغُوا فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** — "that you may seek the bounty of your Lord" — etc., is branched from the light of the day and the darkness of the night, not from what is seen of the spots on the face of the moon and the sun's disk being free from that.

Similar in its failure is the statement of some: that the "sign of the night" is its darkness and the "sign of the day" is its light, and the meaning of "effacing the sign of the night" is the effacing of its darkness by the light of the day, and similarly the effacing of the light of the day by the darkness of the night, and He merely sufficed with mentioning one of them because it points to the other.

The reason for its failure will not be hidden from you if you recall what we indicated previously: that the purpose is to clarify the existence of a difference between the two signs despite their sharing in being signs and indications. The meaning he mentioned nullifies this difference.

His saying: **لِتَبْتَغُوا فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** — "that you may seek the bounty of your Lord" — is branched from His saying: **وَجَعَلْنا آيَةَ النَّهارِ مُبْصِرَةً** — "and We made the sign of the day luminous" — meaning We made it radiant so that you may seek therein provision from your Lord, for provision is His bounty and His gift, the Exalted.

Some mentioned that the implied meaning is: "that you may rest in the night and that you may seek the bounty of your Lord in the day," but "that you may rest in the night" was omitted because He mentioned it in other places. In this is the fact that such an implication contradicts the discourse being directed toward clarifying the consequence of effects upon one of the two signs and not the other, despite both being signs.

His saying: **وَلِتَعْلَمُوا عَدَدَ السِّنِينَ وَالْحِسابَ** — "and that you may know the number of years and the reckoning" — meaning that you may know, through the effacing of the night and the luminosity of the day, the number of years by making a number of days into a single unit upon which a contract is made, and that you may know thereby the reckoning of times and terms. The apparent context is that the knowledge of years and reckoning is branched from making the day luminous, similar to the branching of the seeking of provision upon it. This is because we only become conscious of non-existences (الأعدام) and absences from the direction of existences (الوجودات), not the other way around. Darkness is the absence of light; were it not for light, we would not transition to either light or darkness. Although we utilize both night and day in reckoning and distinguish each by the other outwardly, that which is existential (الوجودي) among them — namely the day — is that to which our sensation first attaches, then we become conscious of that which is non-existential (العدمي) among them — namely the night — by a kind of comparison. Such is the case with every existential thing and the non-existential thing compared to it.

Al-Razi mentioned in his *Tafsir* that it is better for the meaning of "effacing the sign of the night" — according to the view that it is the moon — to be the variation of light that occurs to the moon from one conjunction (المحاق) to the next, by increase and decrease night after night, because of the great effects it has on the seas, the deserts, and the temperaments of people.

The necessary consequence of this — as he indicated — is that His saying: **لِتَبْتَغُوا فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** and His saying: **وَلِتَعْلَمُوا عَدَدَ السِّنِينَ وَالْحِسابَ** are both branched from the effacing of the sign of the night and the making of the sign of the day luminous together. The meaning would be: We made it so that you may seek your provisions through the illumination of the sun and the variation of the moon's light, and that you may also know thereby the years and the reckoning; for the sun is what distinguishes the day from the night, and the moon, through its varying phases, delineates the lunar months, and the months delineate the years. Thus, the "L" (لام) in the two phrases — namely "that you may seek" and "that you may know" — is connected to both verbs "We effaced" and "We made" together.

In this is the fact that the verse is in a context that does not suit making what was mentioned therein of the purpose a purpose resulting from both signs together — namely the effaced sign and the established sign. For you have known that the verses are in the context of reproach and blame, and the verse — namely His saying: **وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ آيَتَيْنِ** — is like a response to what was assumed that man might argue with in his prayer for evil as he prays for good.

Its summary is: that man, because of his haste, does not pay attention to what Allah has sent down of the Book and guided him to of the creed that is most upright. Instead, he plunges into evil and seeks it as he seeks good, without reflecting upon his action or contemplating the aspect of benefit and corruption therein. Rather, he plunges into it as soon as his whim attaches to it and his power facilitates it. He relies in that upon his natural freedom in action, as if he were arguing that Allah empowered him for that and did not prevent him, as Allah narrated from the saying of the polytheists: **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ نَحْنُ وَلا آباؤُنا** — "Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him—neither we nor our fathers..." (16:35).

So it was answered, after being presented in the context of reproach and blame, that the mere attachment of power and the validity of the act do not necessitate the permissibility of the action, nor does His empowering him for both good and evil together indicate the permissibility of plunging into evil like good. For the night and the day are two signs from the signs of Allah in which man lives, yet Allah, the Glorified, effaced the sign of the night and decreed therein stillness and dormancy, and made the sign of the day luminous and perceptive, wherein provision is sought and the number of years and the reckoning are known.

Just as the night and the day sharing in being signs does not necessitate their sharing in movements and fluctuations — for these are for the day specifically — similarly, the sharing of the deeds of good and evil in that they both are realized by the permission of Allah, the Glorified, and are among what Allah has empowered man for equally, does not necessitate the permissibility of his committing both and performing them on an equal footing. Rather, the permissibility of performance and commission is among the properties of the deed of good, not the deed of evil. Thus, it is not for man to follow every path that appears to him, nor to perform everything he craves and to which his whim attaches, relying in that upon what he has been given of natural freedom and Divine empowerment.

From what has preceded, the invalidity of what some mentioned — that the verse is directed toward arguing for monotheism (التوحيد) — becomes clear. For the night and the day, and the variation that occurs to them, and the blessings that result from that, are among the clearest signs of monotheism.

In this is the fact that their indication of monotheism does not necessitate that the purpose be to convey it and argue by them for it in whatever context they occur.

His saying at the end of the verse: **وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَصَّلْناهُ تَفْصِيلاً** — "and We have elaborated everything in detail" (17:12) — is an indication of the distinction between things and that creation does not contain ambiguity or vagueness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ** — "We have fastened every man’s bird [of omen] to his neck" (17:13). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "The 'bird' (الطائر) here is the deed of the human being. It was likened to the bird that passes by on the right (يسنح) and is taken as a good omen, and the bird that passes by on the left (يبرح) and is taken as an ill omen. The *sanih* (السانح) is that which places its right side toward your left, and the *barih* (البارح) is that which places its left side toward your right. The origin of this is that if it was *sanih*, it was possible for the archer [to hit it], and if it was *barih*, it was not possible for him. Abu Zayd said: Everything that runs of a bird, a gazelle, or otherwise, is to them a 'bird'." This is the end of the quote.

In *al-Kashshaf*: "They used to take omens from birds and call it 'driving' (زجراً). When they traveled and a bird passed by them, they would drive it; if it passed by them as a *sanih*, by passing from the left side to the right, they took it as a good omen, and if it passed as a *barih*, by passing from the right side to the left, they took it as an ill omen. Therefore, it was called 'taking an ill omen' (تطيراً)." This is the end of the quote.

It is said in *al-Mufradat*: "So-and-so *tatayyara* (تطير) and *attayyara* (أطير) — its origin is taking omens from birds, then it is used for everything that is taken as a good or ill omen: **قالُوا إِنَّا تَطَيَّرْنا بِكُمْ** — 'They said, "Indeed we take you for an ill omen."' (36:18). For this reason, it was said: 'There is no bird except Your bird' (لا طير إلا طيرك). And He said: **إِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا** — 'if an ill befalls them, they take it as an ill omen' (7:131), meaning they take it as an ill omen. **أَلا إِنَّما طائِرُهُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ** — 'Look! Their ill omen is with Allah' (7:131), meaning their wretchedness is what Allah has prepared for them due to their evil deeds. Upon this is His saying: **قالُوا اطَّيَّرْنا بِكَ وَبِمَنْ مَعَكَ** — 'They said, "We take you and those with you for an ill omen."' (27:47); **قالَ طائِرُكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ** — 'He said, "Your ill omen is with Allah."' (27:47); **قالُوا طائِرُكُمْ مَعَكُمْ** — 'They said, "Your ill omen is with yourselves."' (36:19); **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ** — 'We have fastened every man’s bird to his neck' — meaning his deed that flew from him of good and evil. It is said *tatayaru* (تطايرا) when they hasten, and it is said when they disperse." This is the end of the quote.

In short, the context of what is before the verse and what is after it, especially His saying: **مَنِ اهْتَدى فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ** — "Whoever is guided is only guided for [the good of] his own soul" — etc., provides that what is meant by the "bird" (الطائر) is that which is used to indicate good or ill fortune and reveals the excellence or evil of the final end. Thus, for every human being, there is something connected to the final end of his state, by which its quality of good or evil is known.

The "fastening" (إلزام) of the bird is making it inseparable from him, not leaving him. The fastening was placed on the "neck" (العنق) because it is the limb that a human being cannot be separated from, nor can it be separated from the human being, unlike the extremities such as the hand and the foot. It is the limb that connects the head to the chest, so whatever is hung upon it of a necklace, a collar, or a shackle is the first thing seen when facing a person.

Thus, the meaning of His saying: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ** is that which results in felicity or wretchedness for every human being is with him, not leaving him, by a decree from Allah, the Glorified; for He is the one who fastened it to him. This is the deed that the human being performs, according to His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى وَأَنْ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرى ثُمَّ يُجْزاهُ الْجَزاءَ الْأَوْفى** — "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for, and that he will soon be shown his endeavour, then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward." (53:39-41).

So the "bird" that Allah fastened to the human being in his neck is his deed. The meaning of fastening it to him is that Allah decreed that every deed shall remain with its doer, and its good and evil, its benefit and harm, shall return to him without leaving him for another. It has been inferred from His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ ... إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ** — "And indeed hell is the tryst of them all... Indeed the God-conscious will be amid gardens and springs." (15:43-45) that it is from the decisive decree (القضاء المحتوم) that the excellent end is for faith and godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding against His displeasure* — and the evil end is for disbelief and disobedience.

The necessary consequence of this is that there is with every human being something of his deed that determines for him his state in the final end of his affair, an inseparable accompaniment that does not leave him, and a definitive determination that does not err or mistake, according to what has been decreed: that every deed belongs to its owner, he has nothing but it, and that the destination of obedience is Paradise and the destination of disobedience is the Fire.

Through what has preceded, it becomes clear that the verse only establishes the necessity of felicity and wretchedness for the human being from the direction of his good and evil deeds acquired through the path of choice (الاختيار), without nullifying the effect of the deed on felicity and wretchedness by establishing an eternal decree (قضاء أزلي) that necessitates felicity or wretchedness for the human being whether he acts or does not act, and whether he obeys or disobeys, as some have imagined.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً يَلْقاهُ مَنْشُوراً** — "and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open." (17:13). The state of this book is clarified by His saying after it: **اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ كَفى بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيباً** — "‘Read your book! Today your soul suffices as a reckoner against you.’" (17:14). This indicates, first, that the book brought out for him is his own book, not related to another. Second, that the book encompasses the realities of his deeds that he performed in the world without missing anything of them, as in His saying: **يَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها** — "They will say, ‘Woe to us! What a book is this! It leaves out nothing, small or great, but has counted it.’" (18:49). Third, that the deeds it has counted are manifest therein in their realities of felicity or wretchedness, apparent in their results of good or evil, a manifestation that is not hidden by any veil nor cut off by any excuse. The Exalted said: **لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ** — "‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today.’" (50:22).

It appears from His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ** — "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done..." (3:30) that the book encompasses the deeds themselves in their realities, not just written marks as in the books made among us in the world. Thus, it is the deeds themselves that Allah makes the human being see directly, and there is no proof like direct seeing (العيان).

By this, it becomes clear that what is meant by the "bird" and the "book" in the verse is one thing, which is the deed that the human being performs. However, the Glorified said: **وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً** — "and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book" — thus distinguishing the book from the bird. He did not say: "and We shall bring it out" (ونخرجه), lest it be imagined that the deed only becomes a book on the Day of Resurrection and was before that a bird and not a book, or imagined that the bird is hidden and concealed, not brought out before the Day of Resurrection, which would not suit its being fastened to him in his neck.

In short, in His saying: **وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ** — "and We shall bring out for him" — is an indication that the book of deeds in its realities is hidden from human perception, veiled behind the veil of heedlessness. Allah, the Glorified, only brings it out for the human being on the Day of Resurrection and informs him of its details, which is the meaning of His saying: **يَلْقاهُ مَنْشُوراً** — "he will find it spread open."

In this is an indication that this matter is prepared for him, not neglected. Thus, it is a confirmation of His saying: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ**, because the result is that the human being will inevitably reach the consequence of his deed: first, because it is inseparable from him, not leaving him; and second, because it is written in a book that will appear to him, and he will find it spread open.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ كَفى بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيباً** — "‘Read your book! Today your soul suffices as a reckoner against you.’" (17:14). Meaning, it is said to him: "Read your book," etc.

In His saying: **كَفى بِنَفْسِكَ** — "your soul suffices" — the "B" (بـ) is extra for emphasis (للتأكيد), and its origin is "your soul sufficed" (كفت نفسك). The verb was not made feminine because the subject is metaphorically feminine (مؤنث مجازي), with which both masculine and feminine forms are permissible. It has also been said that it is a verbal noun (اسم فعل) meaning "be content" (اكتف) and the "B" is not extra; and it has been explained otherwise.

In the verse is an indication that the proof of the book is decisive (قاطعة), such that its reader does not doubt it even if he were the criminal himself. And how could he not? For in it is the direct witnessing of the deed itself, and by it is the recompense. The Exalted said: **لا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "‘Do not make any excuses today. You are only being requited for what you used to do.’" (66:7).

It has become clear from what we mentioned regarding the aspect of the connection of His saying: **وَيَدْعُ الْإِنْسانُ بِالشَّرِّ** — "Man prays for evil..." — to what preceded it, the aspect of the connection of these two verses — namely His saying: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ** to His saying **حَسِيباً**. The summary of the meaning of the verses — and the context is one of reproach and blame — is that Allah, the Glorified, sent down the Quran and made it a guide to a creed that is most upright, following the Divine Sunnah in guiding people to monotheism and servitude (العبودية), and bringing felicity to those who are guided and wretchedness to those who stray. However, man does not distinguish good from evil, nor does he differentiate between the beneficial and the harmful; rather, he hastens toward everything he desires, seeking evil as he seeks good.

The reality is that the deed, whether good or evil, is inseparable from its owner, not leaving him. It is also preserved for him in a book that will be brought out for him on the Day of Resurrection and spread open before him, and he will be reckoned for it. Since this is the case, it is obligatory upon the human being not to rush into plunging into everything he desires and craves, nor to hasten to commit it. Rather, he should pause in matters and reflect until he distinguishes between them and differentiates their good from their evil, thus taking the good and avoiding the evil.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَنِ اهْتَدى فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ ضَلَّ فَإِنَّما يَضِلُّ عَلَيْها وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** — "Whoever is guided is only guided for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, goes astray only to its detriment. No bearer shall bear another’s burden." (17:15). It is said in *al-Mufradat*: "**The burden** (الوزر) is the weight, likened to the weight of a mountain. It is used to express sin (الإثم) just as it is expressed by weight. The Exalted said: **لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً** — 'that they may bear their burdens in full' (16:25), like His saying: **وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَأَثْقالاً مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ** — 'They will surely carry their own loads, and [other] loads along with their own loads' (29:13). He said: And His saying: **وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** — 'No bearer shall bear another’s burden' — means she does not carry his burden such that the one whose burden is carried becomes free of it." This is the end of the quote.

The verse is in the position of a result for His saying: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ** — "We have fastened every man’s bird..." — etc. The third sentence, **وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى**, is a confirmation of the second sentence, **وَمَنْ ضَلَّ فَإِنَّما يَضِلُّ عَلَيْها**.

The meaning is: since the deed, whether good or evil, is inseparable from its owner and does not leave him, and it is preserved for its owner and he will witness it at the reckoning, then whoever is guided is only guided for his own soul and his own soul benefits from it without it following another. And whoever strays from the path only strays against his own soul and his own soul is harmed by it without it leaving him to attach to another. No soul that bears a load shall bear the load of another soul, not as might be imagined by the followers of misguidance that if they stray, the consequence of their misguidance is upon their leaders who misled them, and as is imagined by those who blindly follow their fathers and ancestors that their sins and burdens are for their fathers and ancestors and not for them.

Indeed, for the leaders of misguidance is the like of the burdens of their followers, and for the one who establishes an evil custom (سنة سيئة) are the burdens of those who act upon it, and for the one who says: "Follow us and we will bear your sins" are the sins of their sins. However, all of that is the burden of leadership, of establishing the custom, and of bearing the sins, not the very same burden that belongs to the doer such that the deed leaves its doer and attaches to the followed. Rather, if it were the same, its meaning would be that two people are punished for one deed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّى نَبْعَثَ رَسُولاً** — "And We do not punish until We have sent an apostle." (17:15). The apparent context prevailing in the verse and the verses following it — indeed, it and the previous verses — is that what is meant by "punishment" (التعذيب) is worldly punishment through the penalty of extermination (عقوبة الاستئصال). This is supported by the specific context of the negation **وَما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ** (And We were not punishers), where He did not say "and We are not punishers" (ولسنا معذبين), nor "We do not punish" (ولا نعذب), nor "We shall never punish" (ولن نعذب). Rather, He said: **وَما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ**, which indicates the continuity of negation in the past, appearing to mean that the Divine Sunnah in the past, perished nations was established such that He would not punish them except after sending an apostle to them to warn them of Allah's punishment.

This is also supported by the fact that He, the Exalted, referred to this sent one as an "apostle" (رسولاً) rather than a "prophet" (نبياً). We have already mentioned in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume of the book, regarding the difference between prophethood (النبوة) and apostleship (الرسالة), that apostleship is a specific Divine office that results in the decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل) upon the community, either with the punishment of extermination or with enjoyment of life until a specified term. The Exalted said: **وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ** — "There is an apostle for every nation: when their apostle comes, judgment is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged." (10:47), and He said: **قالَتْ رُسُلُهُمْ أَفِي اللهِ شَكٌّ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — "Their apostles said, ‘Is there any doubt about Allah, the originator of the heavens and the earth?! He calls you to forgive you some of your sins, and to grant you respite until a specified time.’" (14:10).

Thus, the expression "apostle" is to convey that what is intended is the negation of worldly punishment, not the punishment of the Hereafter or punishment in an absolute sense.

So His saying: **وَما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّى نَبْعَثَ رَسُولاً** is like a rebuttal to what might be imagined from the previous verses informing of the attachment of the effect of deeds to their owner and the good news for the righteous of a great reward and for the wicked of a painful punishment. This might give the impression that the consequences of evil deeds — encompassing both worldly and after-life punishment — will follow them and overwhelm their owner without restriction or condition.

So it was answered that Allah, the Glorified, by His vast mercy and complete care, does not punish people with the punishment of extermination — which is the punishment of this world — except after sending an apostle to warn them of it. Although He has the right to punish them with it, He, by His mercy and compassion, goes to great lengths in exhortation and completes proof after proof, then sends down the penalty. Thus, His saying: **وَما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّى نَبْعَثَ رَسُولاً** is a negation of the occurrence of punishment, not of its permissibility.

The verse — as you see — is not directed toward endorsing the rational judgment of the "evil of punishment without clarification" (قبح العقاب بلا بيان). Rather, it reveals the requirement of Divine care (العناية الإلهية) that He does not punish a people with the punishment of extermination except after sending an apostle to them, thereby confirming the proof for them and striking them with clarification after clarification.

As for prophethood (النبوة) — *the office of receiving and conveying Divine messages* — by which obligations are conveyed and legislations are clarified, it is that by which Divine accountability and forgiveness are established, and by which reward and punishment in the Hereafter are fixed in matters where truth and falsehood are not clear except through the path of prophethood, such as the subsidiary obligations (التكاليف الفرعية). As for the principles (الأصول) which the intellect is independent in perceiving, such as monotheism, prophethood, and the Return, the effects of their acceptance and the consequences of their rejection attach to the human being by rational establishment (الثبوت العقلي) without depending on prophethood or apostleship.

In short, the principles of religion (أصول الدين) — which the intellect is independent in clarifying and upon which the acceptance of the branches contained in the prophetic call is based — establish Divine accountability for their rejection as soon as the decisive rational proof is established, without depending on the clarification of the prophet and the apostle; for the validity of the clarification of the prophet and the apostle depends upon them, so if they depended upon it, it would be circular.

Accountability in the Hereafter for the branches (الفروع) is established by the prophetic clarification, and the proof is not completed in them by the mere judgment of the intellect. We have detailed the discourse on this in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume, in the stories of Noah in the tenth volume of the book, and elsewhere. Worldly accountability through the punishment of extermination depends on the sending of the apostle by a favor from Allah, the Glorified, not due to a rational judgment that renders this type of accountability impossible before the sending of the apostle, as you have known.

The commentators have long disputes regarding the verse which we have omitted addressing because most of them fall outside the purpose of exegetical research. Perhaps what we have provided of the research does not agree with what they have provided, but the truth is most worthy of being followed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا أَرَدْنا أَنْ نُهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنا مُتْرَفِيها فَفَسَقُوا فِيها فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْناها تَدْمِيراً** — "When We desire to destroy a town, We command its affluent ones [to obey Us]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly." (17:16). Al-Raghib said: "**Affluence** (الترفه) is expansion in blessing. It is said: 'So-and-so was made affluent' (أترف), so he is 'affluent' (مترف)..." — until he said regarding His saying **أَمَرْنا مُتْرَفِيها** — "They are those described by His saying, the Glorified: **فَأَمَّا الْإِنْسانُ إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ رَبُّهُ فَأَكْرَمَهُ وَنَعَّمَهُ** — 'As for man, when his Lord tests him, and honors him, and blesses him...'" (89:15). This is the end of the quote. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "**Affluence** (الترفه) is blessing. Ibn Arafa said: The *mutraf* (المترف) is the one left to do as he wishes and is not prevented from it. And he said: **Utter destruction** (التدمير) is annihilation, and *damar* (الدمار) is annihilation." This is the end of the quote.

**And His saying:** **إِذا أَرَدْنا أَنْ نُهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً** — "When We desire to destroy a town" — meaning when the time of their destruction draws near, similar to their saying: "When the sick person desires to die, such-and-such happens," and "When the sky desires to rain, such-and-such happens," meaning when the time of his death draws near and when the time of its raining draws near. For it is known that he does not desire death in the literal sense of "desire" (الإرادة), nor does it desire raining likewise. In the Quran: **فَوَجَدا فِيها جِداراً يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَنْقَضَ** — "they found in it a wall which was about to collapse" (18:77).

It is possible that what is meant by it is the actual will (الإرادة الفعلية), the reality of which is the agreement of the causes necessitating the thing and their cooperation toward its occurrence. This is close to the first meaning, and its reality is the realization of the causes for their destruction, which is the ingratitude for blessing and tyranny through disobedience, as the Glorified said: **لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ** — "‘If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe.’" (14:7), and He said: **الَّذِينَ طَغَوْا فِي الْبِلادِ فَأَكْثَرُوا فِيهَا الْفَسادَ فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذابٍ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ** — "who rebelled in the cities and caused much corruption in them. So your Lord poured on them the whip of punishment. Indeed your Lord is in ambush." (89:11-14).

His saying: **أَمَرْنا مُتْرَفِيها فَفَسَقُوا فِيها** — "We command its affluent ones. But they commit transgression in it." It is known from His speech, the Exalted, that He does not command disobedience with a legislative command (أمراً تشريعياً); for He is the one who says: **قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ** — "Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency.’" (7:28). As for the generative command (الأمر التكويني) — *the Divine decree that brings things into existence* — its lack of attachment to disobedience from the perspective that it is disobedience is even clearer, because it makes the action necessary, whereby its attachment to human choice is nullified, and there is no disobedience without choice. The Exalted said: **إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is." (36:82).

So the object of the command in His saying: **أَمَرْنا** (We commanded), if it is obedience, then the command is in its literal sense, which is the **legislative command** (الأمر التشريعي). It would be the command directed to them through the tongue of the apostle who conveys to them the command of their Lord and warns them of His punishment if they oppose. This is the matter that is specific to the apostle, as previously indicated. Thus, if they oppose and transgress against the command of their Lord, the word becomes due against them — which is that they are punished if they oppose — so they are annihilated and destroyed utterly.

If the object of the command is transgression and disobedience, then the command is intended to mean the increasing of the outpouring of blessings upon them and providing them abundantly by way of "respite" (الإملاء) and "gradual leading" (الاستدراج), bringing them thereby closer to transgression until they transgress, so the word becomes due against them and the punishment descends upon them.

These are two aspects in the meaning of His saying: **أَمَرْنا مُتْرَفِيها فَفَسَقُوا فِيها**. It is permissible to explain it by either of them, but the first of the two aspects is distant, first, because our saying: "I commanded him and he did" and "I commanded him and he transgressed," its apparent meaning is the attachment of the command to the very thing that was branched from it. Second, there is no apparent reason for the attachment of the command to the affluent ones specifically, while the transgression is for all the people of the town; otherwise, they would not have been destroyed.

It is said in *al-Kashshaf*: "The command is metaphorical (مجاز) because the reality of commanding them to transgress would be to say to them: 'Transgress,' and this does not happen. So it remains that it is metaphorical. The aspect of the metaphor is that He poured blessings upon them abundantly, so they made them a means to disobediences and the following of desires. Thus, it is as if they were commanded to do that because the granting of blessing was a cause for it. He only bestowed them upon them so that they might be grateful and perform good therein, and be enabled for excellence and piety, just as He created them healthy and strong and empowered them for good and evil, and requested from them the preference of obedience over disobedience. But they preferred transgression; so when they transgressed, the word became due against them — which is the word of punishment — so He destroyed them.

If you say: Why did you not claim that its meaning is: 'We commanded them to obedience but they transgressed'? I say: Because the omission of that for which there is no evidence is not permissible. So how can that be omitted for which the evidence stands for its opposite? That is because the commanded object was only omitted because 'they transgressed' (فسقوا) points to it, and it is a widespread form of speech. It is said: 'I commanded him and he stood' and 'I commanded him and he read'; nothing is understood from it except that the commanded object was standing or reading. If you were to go about estimating something else, you would require your interlocutor to have knowledge of the Unseen.

This is not necessitated by their saying: 'I commanded him and he disobeyed me' or 'and he did not comply with my command,' because that is contrary to the command and contradictory to it. That which contradicts the command cannot be commanded, so it was impossible to be intended at all such that it could be made an indicator of the commanded object. Thus, the commanded object in this speech was not indicated nor intended, as if he were saying: 'There was a command from me but there was no obedience from him,' just as the one who says: 'So-and-so gives and withholds, and commands and forbids' is not intending an object.

If you say: Would not the established knowledge that Allah does not command indecency, but rather commands justice and good, be a proof that the meaning is: 'We commanded them with good but they transgressed'?

I say: That is not correct because His saying **فَفَسَقُوا** (but they transgressed) repels it. It is as if you manifested something while you claim to intend its opposite. Thus, turning the expression to the metaphor was the proper way." This is the end of the quote.

This is a fine discourse in approximating the appearance of His saying: **أَمَرْنا مُتْرَفِيها فَفَسَقُوا فِيها** in that the commanded object is the transgression. As for its being explicit in it such that it admits no other possibility as he claims, then no. For why is it not permissible for the verse to be of the pattern of our saying: "I commanded him and he disobeyed me," where the disobedience — which is contrary to the command — is a context (قرينة) indicating that the commanded object is obedience? For transgression (الفسق) and disobedience (المعصية) are one; for **transgression** is the exiting from the state of servitude and obedience, so it is disobedience. The meaning then would be: We commanded its affluent ones with obedience, but they transgressed against Our command and disobeyed it. Or the command in the verse is used in an intransitive sense (استعمال اللازم), and the meaning is: Our command was directed toward its affluent ones, but they transgressed therein against it.

The truth is that both aspects are acceptable, although the second is not without its own apparent strength. The restriction of the command to the affluent ones has been answered by the fact that they are the leaders, the masters, and the followed authorities, and others are their followers; and the ruling of the follower follows the ruling of the followed. This is not without some weakness.

Some mentioned in explaining the verse that His saying: **أَمَرْنا مُتْرَفِيها** etc., is an adjective for "town" (قرية) and is not the response to "when" (إذا). The response to "when" is omitted, similar to His saying: **حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها وَفُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها وَقالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها** — "until when they reach it, and its gates are opened, and its keepers say to them..." (39:73) to the end of the verse, because it is dispensed with by the indication of the discourse.

Others mentioned that in the verse there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير), and the implied meaning is: "And when We commanded the affluent ones of a town and they transgressed therein, We desired to destroy it." This is because there is no meaning to the desire for destruction before the realization of its cause, which is transgression. This is a weak view like the one before it.

All of this is based on the well-known reading **أَمَرْنا** (We commanded) with a *fatha* on the *hamza* and then the *mim* without doubling, from "command" (الأمر) meaning request. It has also been taken from "command" meaning "to increase" (الإكثار), i.e., We increased its affluent ones in wealth and children, so they transgressed therein.

It has been read as *āmar-nā* (آمرنا) with a long vowel, attributed to Ali (peace be upon him), Asim, Ibn Kathir, Nafi', and others. It is from *īmār* (إيمار) meaning the increasing of wealth and progeny, or meaning the obligation of initiating an action. It has also been read as *ammarnā* (أمرنا) with a doubled *mim*, from "appointing as ruler" (التأمير) meaning the granting of authority. This was attributed to Ali, al-Hasan, and al-Baqir (peace be upon them), and to Ibn Abbas, Zayd bin Ali, and others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنَ الْقُرُونِ مِنْ بَعْدِ نُوحٍ وَكَفى بِرَبِّكَ بِذُنُوبِ عِبادِهِ خَبِيراً بَصِيراً** — "How many generations We have destroyed after Noah! Your Lord suffices as one who is aware and observant of His servants’ sins." (17:17). It is said in *al-Mufradat*: "**The generation** (القرن) refers to the people joined together in one time, and its plural is *qurun* (قرون). He said: **وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا الْقُرُونَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ** — 'We have already destroyed the generations before you' (10:13); **وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنَ الْقُرُونِ** — 'How many generations We have destroyed'." This is the end of the quote. The meaning of the verse is clear, and in it is the confirmation of what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the Divine Sunnah unfolding in the destruction of towns, by referring to the past, destroyed generations.

The verse is not without an indication that the Sunnah of destruction was only legislated in the human generations after Noah (peace be upon him), and such is the case. It has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ** — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets..." (2:213) in the second volume of the book, that human society before the time of Noah (peace be upon him) was upon the simplicity of primordial nature, then they differed after that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنا لَهُ فِيها ما نَشاءُ لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنا لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلاها مَذْمُوماً مَدْحُوراً** — "Whoever desires the transitory [life], We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blamed and spurned." (17:18). "The transitory" (العاجلة) is an adjective whose noun is omitted; perhaps its noun is "life" (الحياة) due to the context of its contrast with the Hereafter in the following verse, which is the After-life. It has been said: what is meant is transitory blessings, and it has been said: transitory worldly accidents.

In *al-Mufradat*: "The origin of **entering** (الصلى) is the kindling of fire. He said: Al-Khalil said: 'The disbeliever *saliya* (صلي) the fire' means he endured its heat: **يَصْلَوْنَها فَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ** — 'they shall enter it, and it is an evil destination' (14:29). It has been said: *saliya* the fire means he entered it, and another made him enter it (*aslāhu*): **فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ ناراً** — 'We shall soon make him enter a Fire' (4:30)." This is the end of the quote. In *al-Majma'*: "**Spurning** (الدحر) is distancing, and the **spurned** (المدحور) is the one distanced and driven away. It is said: 'O Allah, spurn the Satan from us,' meaning distance him." This is the end of the quote.

When He, the Glorified, mentioned His Sunnah regarding worldly punishment following the call of apostleship — and that He guides the human nations toward faith and righteous deeds until, if they become corrupt and cause corruption, He sends an apostle to them; then if they rebel and transgress, He punishes them with the punishment of extermination — He returned to clarifying His Sunnah regarding the punishment of the Hereafter and the reward therein in this verse and the two verses after it. He mentions in one verse the criterion for the punishment of the Hereafter, in another verse the criterion for its reward, and in another verse the summary of the discourse and the universal principle (الأصل الكلي) in that.

His saying: «*Whoever desires the fleeting [life]*» (مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعاجِلَةَ) means the one who desires the immediate life, which is the life of this world. Desiring the worldly life is simply seeking its enjoyments, in which the soul finds pleasure and to which the heart becomes attached. Attachment to the fleeting life and seeking it is only considered "seeking it" when it is intended independently (بالاستقلال), not as a means to reach the happiness of the Hereafter. Otherwise, it would be a desire for the Hereafter, for the Hereafter cannot be reached except through the path of this world. Thus, a person is not considered a seeker of this world unless he turns away from the Hereafter and forgets it, such that his will becomes purely devoted to this world. This is also indicated by the specific expression in the verse: «*Whoever desires*» (مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ), as it denotes the continuity of that desire.

This is the person who sees nothing for himself except this material, worldly life and denies the life of the Hereafter.

By doing so, he renders the belief in prophethood and monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — futile, as there is no effect for faith in Allah and His messengers, nor for practicing religion (الدين), without the belief in the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and final judgment*. The Exalted said: **فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** "So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way" (An-Najm: 30).

His saying: «*We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire*» (عَجَّلْنا لَهُ فِيها ما نَشاءُ لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ) means: We hasten to give him what he desires in the world, but not by giving him exactly what *he* wants, but rather what *We* want. Thus, the matter is up to Us, not him, and the effect belongs to Our will, not his. Furthermore, it is not by giving what We give to everyone who desires it, but rather to "whomever We desire." Therefore, the will of individuals does not govern Us; rather, Our will is what governs them.

His actualized will (إرادته الفعلية) for a thing is the convergence of causes (الأسباب) for its being and the realization of the complete cause (العلة التامة) for its appearance. Thus, the verse indicates that while man desires the world, he is provided from it according to what the causes and factors—which Allah has set in motion in the universe and for which He has decreed specific effects—allow him. He attains something of what he desires and asks for through the tongue of his existential state (لسان تكوينه), but he has nothing except what the causes lead him to, and Allah encompasses them from behind.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned this reality in another way in His saying: **وَلَوْ لا أَنْ يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً لَجَعَلْنا لِمَنْ يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفاً مِنْ فِضَّةٍ وَمَعارِجَ عَلَيْها يَظْهَرُونَ وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْواباً وَسُرُراً عَلَيْها يَتَّكِؤُنَ وَزُخْرُفاً وَإِنْ كُلُّ ذلِكَ لَمَّا مَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** "Were it not that mankind would have become a single community, We would have surely provided for those who deny the All-beneficent, roofs of silver for their houses and steps [of silver] whereby they would ascend, and doors [of silver] for their houses and couches [of silver] whereon they would recline, and ornaments [of gold]. Yet all that is nothing but the wares of the life of this world" (Az-Zukhruf: 33-35). This means: were it not for the fact that all people live in a uniform manner under the law of causes and effects (الأسباب والعلل), where there is no difference between the disbeliever and the believer in the face of cosmic causes—rather, whoever encounters the causes of wealth and riches is made wealthy and enriched, whether he be a believer or a disbeliever, and vice versa—We would have singled out the disbelievers with an abundance of worldly blessings, as they have no worth in Our sight nor any value in the market of the Hereafter.

Some have mentioned that the "desire for the fleeting life" refers to desiring it through one's deeds—meaning that one intends the world by his actions rather than the Hereafter, thus being deprived of the Hereafter. However, this is a restriction (تقييد) without a restrictor (مقيد). Perhaps he took this from the saying of the Exalted: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا وَزِينَتَها نُوَفِّ إِلَيْهِمْ أَعْمالَهُمْ فِيها وَهُمْ فِيها لا يُبْخَسُونَ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا النَّارُ** "As for those who desire the life of this world and its glitter, We shall pay them in full for their works therein, and they shall not be defrauded therein. They are the ones for whom there shall be nothing in the Hereafter but the Fire" (Hud: 15-16). But the two verses differ in their purpose (الغرض). The purpose in the verse we are discussing is to explain that the seeker of the world attains nothing but what is from it, whereas the purpose of the verse in Surah Hud is that man attains nothing but his deeds; so if he desires the world, his deeds are paid to him therein. There is a clear difference between the two purposes, so understand that.

His saying: «*Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blamed and spurned*» (ثُمَّ جَعَلْنا لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلاها مَذْمُوماً مَدْحُوراً) means: We have made his recompense in the Hereafter to be Hell, enduring its heat while being blamed and distanced from mercy. These two descriptions imply that he is specifically destined for Hell and deprived of forgiveness and mercy.

Although the verse explains the state of the one attached to the world who forgets and denies the Hereafter, seeking and denial vary in degrees. Some are such in both word and deed, while others are such in deed despite acknowledging it in word. The confirmation of this is the saying of the Exalted that follows: «*and the Hereafter is surely greater in rank*» (وَلَلْآخِرَةُ أَكْبَرُ دَرَجاتٍ).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an effort worthy of it, should he be faithful—the endeavor of such shall be appreciated*» (وَمَنْ أَرادَ الْآخِرَةَ وَسَعى لَها سَعْيَها وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ كانَ سَعْيُهُمْ مَشْكُوراً). Al-Raghib said: **Striving** (السعي) is fast walking, which is less than running, and it is used for diligence in a matter, whether good or evil. (End of the relevant portion).

His saying: «*Whoever desires the Hereafter*» (مَنْ أَرادَ الْآخِرَةَ) means the life of the Hereafter, similar to what was mentioned regarding «*Whoever desires the fleeting [life]*». The discussion regarding those who say it means "whoever desires the Hereafter by his deeds" is similar to the discussion on the previous verse.

His saying: «*and strives for it with an effort worthy of it*» (وَسَعى لَها سَعْيَها). The preposition (اللام) denotes exclusivity (الاختصاص), as does the annexation (إضافة) of "striving" to the pronoun referring to the Hereafter. The meaning is: he strives and exerts himself for the Hereafter with the striving that is specific to it. From this, it is understood that his striving for it must be a striving that befits it and is its right, such as exerting the utmost effort in the excellence of the deed and deriving it from certain intellect (عقل قطعي) or a religious proof (حجة شرعية).

His saying: «*should he be faithful*» (وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ) means a believer in Allah, which necessitates His monotheism and the affirmation of prophethood and the Return. For whoever does not acknowledge one of these three traits is not considered by Allah in His speech as a believer in Him, and verses to this effect are numerous.

Furthermore, the very restriction by the phrase «*should he be faithful*» is sufficient for the aforementioned qualification. For whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with its worthy effort is certainly a believer in Allah and in a realm beyond this worldly realm. If the restriction of faith were not to indicate the necessity of the faith being sound—and part of its soundness is that it be accompanied by monotheism and the affirmation of prophethood—there would be no reason for the restriction. Thus, the mere restriction of faith suffices without the need for assistance from other verses.

His saying: «*the endeavor of such shall be appreciated*» (فَأُولئِكَ كانَ سَعْيُهُمْ مَشْكُوراً) means that Allah appreciates it through His gracious acceptance and praise for the one who strove. His appreciation (شكره) for the servant's deed is a favor upon a favor (تفضل على تفضل). This is because the original rewarding of the servant for his deed is a favor, since it is the servant's duty to worship his Master without any obligation of reward upon Him. Thus, rewarding is a favor, and praising him after the reward is a favor upon a favor, and Allah is the Possessor of Great Bounty.

In these two verses, there is an indication that the causes of the Hereafter—which are the deeds—do not fail to reach their goals, unlike worldly causes. For the Exalted says regarding the one who works for the Hereafter: «*the endeavor of such shall be appreciated*», while He says regarding the one who works for the world: «*We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire*».

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*To these and those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined*» (كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ وَما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً). It is stated in *al-Mufradat*: The root of **extending** (المد) is pulling or drawing. From it comes "duration" (المدة) for an extended time, the "suppuration" (مدة) of a wound, and the "swelling" of a river when another river extends it. [The author says:] "I extended my eyes toward such-and-such," as the Exalted said: «*Do not extend your eyes*» (وَلا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ). And "He extended him in his transgression" (مددته في غيه)... and "I extended the army with reinforcements" (أمددت الجيش بمدد) and "the person with food." He said: "Extending" (الإمداد) is mostly used for what is liked, while "extending" (المد) is used for what is disliked, such as: «*We shall provide them with fruits and meat such as they desire*» (وَأَمْدَدْناهُمْ بِفاكِهَةٍ وَلَحْمٍ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ), «*We shall prolong for him the punishment*» (وَنَمُدُّ لَهُ مِنَ الْعَذابِ مَدًّا), «*He leaves them to flounder in their rebellion*» (وَيَمُدُّهُمْ فِي طُغْيانِهِمْ), and «*their brothers, they draw them into error*» (وَإِخْوانُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِي الغَيِ). (End of the summary).

Thus, "extending" a thing (إمداد الشيء ومده) means adding to it something of its own kind, for example, by which its survival is extended and its existence continues. Were it not for that, it would be cut off, like a spring of water that draws from the source, with water added to it time after time, thereby extending its flow.

Allah, the Glorified, extends man in his deeds, whether he is among those who desire the fleeting life or the Hereafter. For everything upon which the deed depends for its realization—knowledge, will, bodily instruments, active powers, external materials upon which the deed occurs and which the agent manipulates, and the causes and conditions (الأسباب والشرائط) connected to them—all of these are existential matters (أمور تكوينية) in which man has no hand. If all or some were missing, the deed would not occur. Allah, the Glorified, is the one who bestows them by His grace and extends man with them through His bounty. If His bounty were cut off, the agent's deed would be cut off.

Thus, the people of the world in their world and the people of the Hereafter in their Hereafter draw from His bounty. Nothing returns to Him, the Glorified, in His bounty except praise. For what He gives is a blessing (نعمة) upon man, provided he uses it well in a place pleasing to his Lord. But if he transgresses by not using it therein and distorts the word from its proper place, he should blame none but himself, while praise belongs to Allah for His beautiful handiwork, and His is the conclusive argument.

His saying: «*To all We extend*» (كُلًّا نُمِدُّ) means We extend both groups: those for whom things are expedited and those whose striving is appreciated. The object (المفعول) is placed before its verb to emphasize it in the discourse, as the intent is to explain the universality of the extension to both groups together.

His saying: «*To these and those*» (هؤُلاءِ وَهَؤُلاءِ) means those for whom things are expedited and those whose striving is appreciated, as each has their own specific attribute. The meaning boils down to the fact that both groups are under Divine upbringing (التربية الإلهية); He bestows His bounty upon them without distinction, except that one uses the Divine blessing to seek the Hereafter, so Allah appreciates his striving, while the other uses it to seek the fleeting life and forgets the Hereafter, so nothing remains for him therein except misery and disappointment.

His saying: «*from the bounty of your Lord*» (مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ) indicates that everything they benefit from in their deeds, as previously mentioned, is not of their own making nor that of any other creature; rather, Allah, the Glorified, is the Originator and Owner of these things, so they are from His bounty.

From this restriction, the reason for the mentioned recompense for each group is understood. Since their deeds were through His extension from His pure bounty, it is only right that he who uses His blessing in disbelief and transgression should enter the Fire, blamed and spurned, and he who uses it in faith in Him and obedience to Him should have his striving appreciated.
